name: Square Mile Sovereignty
description: Square Mile Sovereignty, a conspiracy theory primarily focused on the City of London (the 'Square Mile'), posits that this geographically small area operates as an independent, sovereign state within the United Kingdom, largely outside the control of British Parliament. Proponents argue that the City, governed by the Corporation of London, possesses unique legal privileges and financial power that allow it to dictate national and international policy. This theory often intertwines with anti-Semitic tropes and critiques of global finance. Originating from critiques of the banking system and historical analyses of the City's charters, the theory gained traction in the late 20th and early 21st centuries through alternative media and online forums. Evidence cited includes the City's separate flag, its Lord Mayor, and its historical relationship with the Crown. Mainstream historians and legal scholars dismiss the theory as an exaggeration and misinterpretation of the City's historical privileges and administrative structures. They point out that the City is subject to UK law, and its powers are delegated by Parliament. While the City holds significant financial influence, this influence is attributed to its role as a global financial center, not to any inherent sovereignty.
subtitle: City of London's alleged independence.

claims.claim: The City of London operates as an independent state.
claims.evidence: The City has its own flag, Lord Mayor, and ancient rights.
claims.criticism: These are civic symbols and historical privileges, not evidence of sovereignty. The City is subject to UK law.

claims.claim: The City controls British and international finance.
claims.evidence: Major financial institutions are headquartered in the City.
claims.criticism: The City is a significant financial hub, but its influence is based on market forces, not absolute control. Regulatory bodies exist to oversee these institutions.

claims.claim: The Corporation of London is a secretive and unaccountable body.
claims.evidence: The Corporation has ancient charters and traditions.
claims.criticism: The Corporation's activities are subject to scrutiny, and it is accountable to its electorate and the wider public. It's a local government body with specific responsibilities.

keyFigures.name: David Icke
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the theory, linking it to reptilian conspiracies.

keyFigures.name: Nicholas Shaxson
keyFigures.role: Author critiquing the City's role in tax havens, though not endorsing full sovereignty.

keyFigures.name: Alasdair Nairne
keyFigures.role: Historian who debunks claims of the City's absolute sovereignty.

timeline.event: William the Conqueror grants the City special privileges.
Establishment of the Bank of England within the City.
Theory gains popularity in alternative media.

sources.author: Nicholas Shaxson
sources.work: Treasure Islands: Uncovering the Damage of Offshore Banking and Tax Havens
sources.title: Nicholas Shaxson

sources.author: Alasdair Nairne
sources.work: City of London: Its History, Politics and Society
sources.title: Alasdair Nairne
